description_long: This is a rectangular slab of stone, predominantly gray in color, with a striking fossilized fern pattern embedded on one of its broad faces. The fossil impression is dark and detailed, with delicate leaflets and a central stem clearly visible, suggesting a preserved ancient plant. The stone itself appears to be sedimentary, with a flat, smooth surface on the fossil side and a slightly rougher, more irregular texture on the reverse. There are a few small colored labels or tags affixed to the fossil side, likely for cataloging or identification purposes. The edges of the stone are uneven and natural, with some minor chipping and color variation, including hints of beige and brown. The overall appearance is that of a museum-quality fossil specimen, likely used for educational or display purposes.
description: A rectangular gray stone slab featuring a detailed fossilized fern pattern on one face, with natural edges and minor color variations.
description_view_0: The broad face displays a prominent dark fossilized fern pattern with visible leaflets and attached catalog labels.
description_view_1: A narrow side view showing the thinness of the slab and the rough, uneven edge profile.
description_view_2: The reverse side is mostly plain, with subtle beige and brown discolorations and no visible fossil pattern.
description_view_3: Another narrow side view, similar to view 1, highlighting the slab's thinness and irregular edge.
attribute_location: natural history museum exhibit or educational park
attribute_placement: set as a standalone feature on a display plinth or embedded in a low wall
attribute_pattern: Distinct fossilized fern pattern with regular, repeating leaflets and a central stem, contrasting against the plain stone background.
attribute_weathering: Smooth and well-preserved on the fossil side, with minor edge chipping and some surface discoloration indicating moderate age but limited outdoor exposure.
attribute_integration_with_surroundings: Contrasts sharply with modern urban materials but may be integrated into educational displays or naturalistic landscaping, standing out due to its fossil pattern and catalog labels.
